speaker: doctor
text: Good morning, Mr. Thompson. Let's start with your vitals — your heart rate today is 93 beats per minute, which is slightly elevated but within acceptable limits for now.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your recent labs and imaging, we’ve confirmed the diagnosis of Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 3. This means your kidneys are moderately reduced in function, but with careful management, we can slow progression.

speaker: doctor
text: Your blood pressure reading today is 126 over 78 — the systolic value of 126 mmHg is well-controlled, which is reassuring given your kidney condition.

speaker: doctor
text: You're currently on Amlodipine 10 mg once daily for blood pressure control. That’s appropriate for now, but we’ll revisit dosing if your BP trends upward or if renal function changes.

speaker: patient
text: Thanks, Doctor. I’ve been taking the Amlodipine every morning as prescribed, though I did forget once last week. Also, I’ve noticed more fatigue lately — especially after walking just a few blocks.

speaker: doctor
text: I see your fasting blood glucose came back at 13.8 mmol/L — that’s significantly elevated and suggests uncontrolled diabetes, which is likely contributing to your kidney disease progression.

speaker: patient
text: Oh — that’s higher than my home meter usually shows. I checked it this morning before breakfast, and it was 9.2. But yesterday afternoon, right after lunch, it was 15.1 — so maybe timing matters?

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level is 123 g/L — that’s mildly low, consistent with anemia of chronic disease, which commonly accompanies CKD Stage 3. We’ll monitor this closely and may consider iron studies or erythropoietin support if it drops further.

speaker: patient
text: Anemia? That might explain the fatigue — I’ve been feeling winded even when just folding laundry. And my ankles have been a little puffy the past few days.

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, edema and fatigue align with both anemia and possible fluid retention from CKD. Let’s check your albumin and electrolytes next — especially potassium and creatinine — to assess for volume status and filtration changes.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also had some nausea in the mornings — not vomiting, just queasy — and my appetite’s been off. Could that be related?

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important — nausea and poor appetite can signal worsening uremia in CKD. We’ll add a basic metabolic panel and possibly a urinalysis with ACR to evaluate proteinuria and acid-base balance.

speaker: patient
text: Okay. And about the high sugar — I’ve been trying to cut carbs, but it’s hard. My sister says her doctor put her on metformin, but I heard that’s risky with kidney issues?

speaker: doctor
text: You’re absolutely right to be cautious — metformin isn’t safe with your current eGFR. Instead, I’m going to start you on dapagliflozin 10 mg daily. It’s an SGLT2 inhibitor proven to protect both kidneys and heart in CKD patients with diabetes.

speaker: patient
text: Dapagliflozin — okay, I’ll get that filled. Should I still take the Amlodipine?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — continue Amlodipine for now. We’ll reassess in 4 weeks, especially since dapagliflozin may lower your BP further. Watch for dizziness when standing, and let me know if you develop genital itching or frequent UTIs — those are possible side effects.

speaker: patient
text: Got it. And that hemoglobin number — 123 — is that the same one from my lab last month? Because I think it was 125 then, so it’s dropped a bit.

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, that’s correct — the result from last time was 125 g/L, so this 123 reflects a small decline. Not alarming yet, but it reinforces why we need to address the anemia proactively alongside your glucose and BP control.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, when I checked again this morning before coming in, my blood sugar was 14.3 — so higher than the 9.2 I mentioned earlier. Maybe stress affects it too?

speaker: doctor
text: Stress absolutely raises glucose — cortisol does that. Let’s set up a 7-point glucose log for next visit, and I’ll refer you to our diabetes educator for carb-counting and timing strategies tailored to CKD.
